attachment.content:
- mapping-lint: 审查 mapping/——机械检查破链 (broken link) / 孤儿 (orphan) / 死端 (dead end)，语义判断矛盾 (contradiction) / 墓碑 (tombstone) / 缺交叉引用 (missing cross-reference)。使用场景：（1）用户说「lint the mapping」、「审一下 mapping」、「查破链」、「巡检」、「过一遍 mapping」；（2）用户怀疑 mapping 有积压问题（孤儿页、过时论断 (stale claim)、概念漂移 (concept drift)）；（3）周期性主动巡检（ingest 完一批后）。
- base-query: 通过 Obsidian CLI 只读查询笔记的操作手册：给定一个 `.base` 文件，返回满足其过滤条件的笔记路径清单。所有查询 base 的需求都由 `dp-base` subagent 承接，主对话不直接触发。subagent 启动时预载本手册，按手册执行 `obsidian base:query` 并把路径清单回传上游。
- mapping-ingest: 把源料页面吸收进认知地图（mapping）。
- base-author: 编写和修改 Obsidian Base 文件（`.base`）的操作手册。创建 base、调整过滤器和公式、修改列与分组排序，这些工作都由 `dp-base` subagent 承接，主对话不直接触发。subagent 启动时预载本手册，按四轴设计框架与 Step 1-4 骨架执行。filter、formula、view 的语法参考见 `references/`。
- dp-search: 用 Obsidian 全文搜索的查询语言（boolean / scope / property / 比较 / 正则 / 大小写）做进阶检索。最常见的触发是 LLM 在 moving 阶段做范围检索需要操作符组合：限定到某子树、按 frontmatter 属性筛、找未链接提及 (unlinked mention)、按行 / 块 / 段邻近度匹配、按 task 状态筛、用 regex 抓结构模式。使用场景：（1）单关键词 `obsidian search:context` 命中过多 / 过少需要操作符细化；（2）需要按 frontmatter 属性 / tag / 任务状态 / 路径范围筛；（3）需要按行 / 块 / 段的邻近度匹配；（4）需要 regex 抓结构（wikilink 模式、块 id、日期）。**简单单关键词搜索仍走 raw `obsidian search:context query="<text>" path=...`，不必经本 skill**。
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the current diff and apply the fixes — equivalent to /code-review --fix.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.prompt:
人类问题：「强化学习的泛化能力如何？」

请在 mapping/ 下用 L1/L2 检索找出可能的 POI 落点。关注：
- 强化学习相关的 entity / concept / synthesis
- 泛化（generalization）相关概念
- 可能相关的模型训练范式、SFT vs RL 之类的对比
- 涌现能力、过拟合、分布外（OOD）等关联概念

返回带理由的落点清单。

content.text:
验证完毕。这 5 个文件里的「训练 / 涌现」都是别的语义：「两种智能观」讲的是军事训练士兵，「agi-叙事光谱」讲人类智能被训练成工具，「工程思维」与「组织创新」的「涌现」指群体行为涌现，全部与机器学习语境的强化学习、泛化、涌现能力无关。`anthropic.md` 提到了「训练」「编程能力」但只字未提 RL 或泛化。

最终回传。

未找到相关落点。

检索过程：L1 层枚举了 `mapping/entities`（22 个文件）、`mapping/concepts`（20 个文件）、`mapping/syntheses`（2 个文件）的全部文件名，无任何 RL、reinforcement、generalization、SFT、RLHF、OOD、过拟合相关命名。obsidian aliases verbose 也无相关别名。L2 层在最可疑的 `andrej-karpathy.md` 上分别搜了 `reinforcement|强化学习|RLHF|RL` 与 `generaliz|泛化|out.of.distribution|OOD|过拟合|overfit`，零命中。整个 mapping/ 全局搜同样关键词，零命中。退而搜 `训练|微调|涌现|emergent` 后命中的 5 个文件（`两种智能观.md`、`agi-叙事光谱.md`、`anthropic.md`、`工程思维.md`、`组织创新优先于技术创新.md`）经 `-C 2` 上下文片段核验，「训练」指军事或人力意义上的训练，「涌现」指群体行为涌现，均非机器学习语境，与「强化学习的泛化能力」主题不构成有效落点。

当前 mapping/ 没有覆盖强化学习这一主题域。建议你直接到源料 `Clippings/` 与 `notes/` 下检索，或确认本轮是否需要先 ingest 相关源料再做 orienting。

toolUseResult.stdout: Library/Towards Research of the Intellectual Development of the Child.md

content.text:
源料库这边也只命中 Galperin 那篇儿童智力发展的论文，里面的 generalization 是发展心理学意义上的「概括」，不是机器学习的泛化，构不成有效落点。

结论：[[digital-playground|Digital Playground]] 当前的 mapping/ 与源料目录都没有覆盖机器学习意义上的强化学习。按 [[CLAUDE.md]] 「不根据派生页作答」「在原始上下文中理解」的约束，我没有可读的源料来支撑回答这个问题。

可选的下一步：

1. 你用 Web Clipper 剪一两篇 RL 泛化相关的源料（例如 Kirk et al. 的 *A Survey of Generalisation in Deep RL*、Cobbe 的 ProcGen 工作、近期 RLHF / RLVR 泛化讨论）到 `Clippings/`，再问。
2. 你直接给我源料路径或 URL，我读完再答。
3. 你说「不必查，直接答」，我用通识作答，但答案不会落在 mapping 网络里。

你想走哪条？

lastPrompt: 强化学习的泛化能力如何？

message.content: Galperin 那篇儿童智力发展的论文为什么不构成有效落点？

content.text: 我刚才的判断只基于标题里「Intellectual Development of the Child」，没真读过这篇论文。让我去看一下，确认 generalization 在 Galperin 那里到底指什么。

input.command: grep -n -i -E "generaliz|强化|reinforc|学习|learn" "Library/Towards Research of the Intellectual Development of the Child.md" | head -60
input.description: 在 Galperin 论文里搜关键词

content.content:
33:The study of the intellectual development of the child was hitherto limited to cross-section methods that allowed only to show the level of development achieved. We did not know how concepts are formed in the child; we just gave him tasks requiring the formation of a concept and then found out what he could perform. The method of determining the "zone of proximal development" by the difference in the results between independent action and joint child-and-adult activity merely complicated the scheme, without removing this basic limitation. By building up a serial row of such static indexes, we traced the curve of intellectual development, while its dynamic forces and its very necessity remained concealed. Taking into account various circumstances, one could have very different conceptions about these things. Such conceptions were in fact suggested, for example, by Vygotsky ( 1935 ) and Piaget ( 1953 ). Vygotsky said that "learning pushes ahead leading development in its wake", while Piaget asserts that intellectual development takes place spontaneously and that an efficient learning is possible only on the basis and in the measure of the level of development reached. The possibility of such a difference shows up clearly that the problem of method is the very first problem in studying the intellectual development of the child. In this perspective, we have better start with the description of how our method (Galperin, 1959 ) was formed and how it brought us one day to face the problem of mental development of the child, a subject we had been dodging stubbornly all the time.
45:## THREE BASIC TYPES OF LEARNING
47:After the investigations of W. Kohler ( 1917 ), we can no longer doubt that reasoning in action has completely objective features. Its measure is found in how much the child is orienting the action by its substantial, external, objective conditions. Naturally, we wished to ensure reasoning in action as much as possible and we came, one day, so near to the solution of this problem that we obtained an altogether new type of process. The trials and errors, so characteristic of the traditional formation of actions, have become rare and incidental, the duration of formation has been sharply curtailed (at the expense of most protracted and difficult period of establishing the right action), the deviations of quality in separate executions have become insignificant; transfer has brown substantially, even the attitude of the individual towards the process of learning has changed. The new way of forming actions and concepts was named Type II, in order to mark its difference from the usual, commonly known Type I which historically came first. A comparison between Type I and Type II conditions will easily explain, at the same time, the mechanisms of Type I and its hitherto predominance in learning : the incompleteness of conditions required for the correct performance of a new assignment makes it unavoidable and even advantageous in a way. At the same time, another type of instruction, Type III, was discovered; some of its details will be discussed later.
49:This is how the theory of the three basic types of mental action and concept formation was elaborated. Its most general assumption is that the type of orientation in a matter, the process of learning, the quality of its results, the attitude toward the matter and the process of learning are closely intertwined and form an unitary process, the process of learning. The decisive part in this process is the type of orientation in the matter; that is why we say "type of orientation" for meaning the corresponding type of learning and "type of learning" for meaning the corresponding type of orientation. The theory of basic types of learning is so important for the "learning and development" problem that we feel justified to discuss it at some length. To simplify the explanation, let us introduce some notions (Galperin, 1966 ).
55:***Type I :*** $Oa < Sp$. This means that the individual lacks some of the signs that are essential for the correct performance of a new action; he is unable to reconstruct them without help; if he well carries out such a reconstruction, the formation turns out into Type II. There where the signs are missing, the individual has only got one way open to him, that of blind trials. In this case, errors are unavoidable, and yet incidental success is possible. These successful actions, however, will be noticed only for their result, for often they are far from the best and, what is more important, they will not always lead to the discovery of their objective conditions. That is why successful actions, purposeful as they are, remain, for most part, unreasoned. Correct actions are formed slowly; the process is clearly divided into two phases of unequal duration : the phase in which the general structure of the action is established and the phase in which the action is perfected. In the long run, the action may reach its utmost precision, limited only by physiological possibilities, but as much as a considerable part of its objective conditions remains unknown to the individual, it is highly subject to changes with the even unimportant changes in conditions. This is the origin of fluctuations of quality that are permanently observed in individual performance and out of proportion with the changes in conditions and that also the image, as it were, of spontaneous fluctuations in the characteristics of individual executions. The degree of success in learning greatly varies among the individuals. A transfer to new assignments is very unsatisfactory in a majority of individuals. Since separate executions and the formation of action on the whole are mainly controlled by the final result, the attitude of the individual towards the process of learning is determined by his attitude towards this result and is of purely utilitarian nature.
57:***Type II :*** $Oa = Sp$. In this case, the individual has a complete system of signs at his disposal to perform correctly a new assignment which he follows without failure; otherwise, the return to Type I occurs. Thus the first, longest and most difficult period of acquisition of the action becomes unnecessary. This means that the process of learning is already curtailed considerably. In Type II, every link of the action is correlated to its objective condition, and *in this measure* the action is not only successful, but also reasoned. Very soon it reaches the same physiological possibilities, but now the characteristics of separate performances are far more stable, because the objective signs of the action are very prominent among all other environmental components. The degree of success in learning among the individuals becomes more uniform; group success shows greater uniformity at a higher level. Transfer grows considerably, compared to that in Type I. It shows much fluctuation in different assignments, but even at its best it remains by far incomplete. Since the conditions of the correct performance are given to the individual, his interest is growing into a successful and ever more perfect execution of the assignment, in the very process of learning. This is precisely an interest in the success of his action only, as his attitude towards its result and towards learning in general remains utilitarian. But the final result loses its importance as the only criterion, while the apparent significance of singling out the conditions of action and therefore, of theoretical research, is met with increasing recognition.
59:The advantages of the Type II, as compared to Type I are quite evident and important, especially when the task is limited to mastering separate assignments. But at school, learning assignments usually refer to a certain sphere and come in a long succession. In respect for such a succession, the basis short-coming of Type II learning becomes clearly indicated : the $Oa$ for *every* new assignment has to be indicated from the beginning (transfer remains substantially incomplete); it is to be found empirically, by finding out how much the suggested signs will eliminate errors made by the individuals. This shortcoming of Type II led to the new task of teaching the child how to construct a complete $Oa$ for new assignments, at least for a definite and limited sphere. Evidently, the desired result will be obtained when the child is provided with such a method of analysis to construct, without assistance, a complete $Oa$ for any phenomenon in this sphere. It is also clear that such an analysis must bear on — ( 1 ) the "basic units" of the material of the sphere in question and — ( 2 ) the general rules of their combination in concrete phenomena. In accordance with this, when faced with the first objects from a new sphere, the individual acquires two methods : the method of singling out the basic units from the concrete objects and the method of caracterizing their combinations in these objects. The results is a complete $Oa$ of the object at hand which is different from the empirically constructed complete $Oa$ of Type II, by its rational origin. Such a complete and rational $Oa$ becomes the basis for the acquisition of actions and concepts related to the studied object.
61:## LEARNING BY THE METHOD OF TYPE III
63:The kind of instruction which we called Type III was successfully implemented at first in teaching writing of letters and words. Even in comparison with Type II, the results were proved to be so much better in all respects that we decided to generalize it in applying this type of instruction to the considerably different material : grammatical analysis of words, initial arithmetical operations and concepts.
67:In teaching writing of letters, the contour was chosen as the object to be studied and mastered, or, to be more exact, its shape, size and localization (Galperin, 1958 ). We took the "line of unchanging direction" as unit of contour : at points where the line or its direction began to change, division dots were put on so that the line was located between two such dots. The localization of the dots themselves was indicated at the crosspoints of the "slanting" line with the "reclining" one (the vertical and horizontal lines of the page). Six-year-old children were taught to find the division dots, to supply them with *verbal* characteristics (by describing their localization on the network of coordinates in the page), to transfer the key dots, in accordance with these verbal characteristics, to the clean space on the line and then, following the dots, to reproduce the contour. All these actions requiring orientation in the structure and localization of the contour were transferred by stages to the mental plane where they were brought up to the level of automatism. After that, the children were writing letters and words without thinking of how they had done it. After such a learning for the first few letters, the children mastered independently the writing of the rest of the letters of the Russian alphabet (totalizing 58 letters, capital letters included). During control tests, they had no difficulty in analyzing and reproducing latin letters, Armenian and Arabic writing, shorthand characters, drawings, sketches; they could trace trajectories described by only moving bodies on a plane. Due to the clear arrangement of objects in space, they could count things much better. In a word, transfer was not only complete in the first selected sphere (writing of Russian letters) but reached far beyond its borders covering all the assignments where it was important to take into account the arrangement of objects on a plane. The method of teaching is itself noteworthy. The child was asked not only to show, but also to prove that the line changes at this point exactly and not at an adjacent one, to mark the place of a key dot by drawing auxiliary lines if necessary, and to describe verbally in such a way that another child could indicate its exact localization, etc. In all instances, we resorted to the judgement of other children and asked the individual to describe each element so that it would have the same meaning for all. In other words, the children were systematically taught to develop an objective awareness of the plane structure and of the characteristics of its elements, and to use this knowledge for the recognition and reproduction of concrete graphical objects.
73:This analysis, carried out on different words from different parts of speech, yields a general scheme of the word, and of all the words, which is a common carrier of a somewhat intricate, but clearly outlined system of possible combinations for different units of information. This scheme becomes an instrument of speech activity, while speech itself turns into an active process of using different semes for the desired presentation of things. In this meaning, language becomes indeed not only an instrument of activity, but activity itself (it is Humboldt's idea, though without Humboldt's mysticism). It is noteworthy that this way of understanding the language, *i.e.*, from the aspect of people's consciousness fixed in it, "language consciousness", simplifies considerably the task of characterizing language phenomena and makes it possible to present them with more regularity. This, in its turn, provides an opportunity to ensure a correct orientation among these phenomena and their mastering by means achieving grammatical tasks. This way of correct grammar is directly applied to written and oral speech, without satiety and with ever increasing interest. The initial and basic principle of discrimination of the smallest units of information leads in an easily understood way to a quick and assured formation of the "feeling of language" which can be extensively transferred to the learning of foreign languages, the study of literature and precise formulation of logical thought. This way of learning a language is not merely acquisition of the rules of grammatically correct speech, but also discovery of a new world, the world of people's consciousness concerning things, people and their relations. It is, at the same time, a new structure of speech thinking supported by the basic patterns which have been deposited in this language consciousness.
75:## THE IIId TYPE IN LEARNING OF FIRST MATHEMATICAL CONCEPTS
77:The application of the Type III of orientation to the initial primary, learning of numbers and arithmetical actions deserves a more detailed discussion as this we had when facing the problems of "learning and development" (Galperin, 1959, 1960 ). The key point in primary arithmetics is the concept of unity because it is the basis on which all other numbers and numerical operations are constructed. For a long time, we have been using the traditional definition of unity as separate thing, but now the requirement of rationality (justification of an action by the properties of its object) led us to revise this question radically.
79:For the person who begins to be acquainted with arithmetics, a number always denotes the result of measuring¹. Consequently, the learning of arithmetics must start with measuring. So we began by demonstrating to six-year-old children the importance of measuring for various spheres of life : we took them on shopping tours where they watched how foodstuffs were weighed, shoes, hats and clothes tried on, fabrics measured, etc. Back to school, we told them : "You see, measuring is very used everywhere; let us start measuring too. What are we going to measure ? Well, everything : tables, windows, doors, anything... What shall we measure with ? It depends on what we want to measure. If we want to measure length, we must use something long, if we want to measure water or sand we must use a spoon, a cup, a bucket (*i.e.*, something that has capacity) and if we want to measure weight, we must use something heavy as a weight. We cannot measure water (capacity) by using a string (length), or weight, by using a cup or a spoon. In a word, every property of a thing can be measured only with its own measure. It is why it is very important to understand which of the properties of the thing has to be measured, which one is asked about, as the same thing has various properties". The ability to point
81:¹ We were happy to learn afterwards that the Academician Henri Lebesgue ( 1938 ) thought it was possible to build basic kinds of numbers by measuring and that the Academician A.N. Kolmorogorov stressed, in his foreword to the Russian translation of Lebesgue's book, that measuring is the only strictly scientific method of obtaining numbers in the process of instruction.
83:out the property in question (what is of which one is there more, less or equal quantity ?) was the matter of careful learning. At the same time, children were taught to carry out the measuring procedure. At first, they handled the measure carelessly : now it was undersized, then it was oversized, sometimes there were gaps between two measures, sometimes the measured parts overlapped. Appealing to the judgement of other children, we pointed out these irregulatiries and showed that the result was also incorrect.
87:Then the children were taught to compare the multiplicities they had obtained. They were offered disorderly two rather large groups of objects (of 15 to 20 pieces each) used as marks and that differed in their number only by 1 or 2 pieces (visually this difference could not be established, and the children did not know how to count). The E. asked whether these groups were the same or any of the groups was larger than the other. At first, the children answered at random and, of course, the answers were different. But the E. asked : "How can we prove to all who is right" ? If the children found difficult to answer the question, the E. showed them the method of two mutually correlated (horizontal) rows. This became the chief method of the quantitative comparison of multiplicities and, from them too, of values. Such an arrangement of rows easily led to the formation of concepts like "the same quantity, equal", "more - less", "more by" (and "less by") "so many" elements. Only after such a preliminary learning, we introduced the first concrete number, the unity. It was given the definition of something that was measured and was equal to its measure. Here we stressed the relation of the unity's equality with its own (and only its own) measure : if a different measure was used, the former unity was no longer unity. The following three numbers, 0, 2, 3, were constructed by the E. in accordance with the latent rule $n \pm 1$, after which the rule itself was introduced as a double rule : every following number is greater by 1, every number preceding the one which is indicated is less by 1. Following theses rules, the children by themselves constructed new multiplicities (numbers), while the E. told them how these numbers were called and written. The children did not know yet how to write and used the figures that were printed on cards. Then they performed various actions with every emerging number.
119:At about the same time, we observed an interesting fact already discovered by L.F. Obukhova ( 1966 ). In the middle of the period of learning (Type III), the children carried out the measuring of objects they had to compare only during the first phase of the experiment, when the objects were really equal and appeared equal to the eyes, whereas during the second phase of the experiment, after that one of the objects had been changed, they dispensed with measuring them. Why did they measure things when it was not needed, and why did not they measure things when it would have been useful ? From the point of view of objective results, such a behavior does not appear rational, but it is completely justified if it is considered psychologically (*i.e.*, as a means of strengthening the still shaky idea of the separate basic properties of a thing and of the parameter by which the comparison is to be carried out), if this perceptually weak parameter in perception has to be supported and singled out from the general, global impression which is still dominant in the perceptual field. This middle stage of co-existence, if we may speak so, of a direct perception and of the new mental plane is followed by the final stage, in our experimental teaching, in which the mental plane becomes prevailing over the visual image. The children no longer feel any contradiction between what "seems" and what "really is". The thing which changes in the second phase of Piaget's experiment is immediately limited to one parameter which is clearly distinguished by the children from the parameter of the question : the level of water in the vessel has changed, but not the quantity of water; the distance between the bits has changed, but not their number, and so on.
121:## THE CONNECTION BETWEEN LEARNING AND INTELLECTUAL DEVELOPMENT
123:Thus, the Type III of learning leads to three fundamental changes in the image of things : instead of "just a thing", we now have a complex of relatively independent parameters; instead of "just a property", we have a multitude of "basic units"; instead of a disorderly multitude of parts, we have an organized system of units, the pattern of which is common to all the objects included in the studied sphere. Such a change of the naively egocentrical image does not take place only in mathematics. In the grammatical study of a word, it is also necessary at first to separate the lexical meaning of this word from its proper linguistic meaning; the latter is then presented as a multiplicity of semes, *i.e.* basic units of the language as a means of information, and, finally, a pattern of word is established which is common to all the words of any part of speech. In the calligraphic study of letters, the first thing to do is, so to say, to separate the topological aspect from the dimensional, properly graphical, content of the contour; then the graphical contour is presented as a multiplicity of pieces of line, whereas the coordinate network of the page forms a general pattern within which every contour is provided with its individual localization on the right basis line.
125:The three patterns establish the rational structure of empirical objects. But they do not deal only with the structure of objects. At very stage of the process of emergence, these patterns serve as instruments in solving the problem related to objects under study. When he uses these instruments, the child moves along the lines of these patterns and learns how to follow them in different directions, thus mastering the patterns themselves. In this way, they become also patterns of thinking about things, *i.e.*, general patterns on the basis of which some theoretical actions are undertaken and achieved, *i.e.*, new structures of thought. These structures condition the voluntary logical thinking : instead of blindly following the dominating stimulation, the subject follows a situationally weak but problematically important property; instead of seeing its quality with surprise, he transforms it into a quantity which opens a way to action; instead of being stopped by a disorderly multitude, he organizes it according to a general pattern which allows him to find ways to the solution of a problem.
127:If the Type III of orientation is followed, concrete knowledge about facts and laws of the sphere under study, is formed on the basis of these general patterns : in arithmetics, the knowledge of concrete numbers and of actions performed with them; in morphology, the separate groups and categories of words and the rules according to which they change and agree; in calligraphy, the peculiarities of individual letters, the way by which they are written separately or combined with other letters, and so on. But such a relationship between concrete knowledge and general patterns is established only in the Type III of learning. In the other types of learning (Type I and even Type II), concrete knowledge about facts and laws is assimilated without any connection with these general patterns and, for the most part, without any idea about them. This is why, outside the Type III of learning, the knowledge of facts and even laws does not directly influence the development of thought. Of course, these general patterns are present, but in a hidden manner, in the knowledge acquired through the Type I and Type II. But in this condition, the practical application of knowledge can result in a spontaneous, "intuitional" approximation to these general patterns and in the correspondingly spontaneous reorganization of thought. But it occurs for different individuals with varying degree of success, and even when thought begins to follow these patterns in some degree, they are still treated in a subconscious manner and remain within the framework of the empirical material from which they emerged.
129:Thus all acquirements of learning can be divided into two unequal parts. The first one consists of new general patterns of things which condition a new way of seeing things and thinking about them; the other one consists of concrete facts and laws found in the sphere under study, that is to say, the concrete material of a science. In its totality, the second part has more weight than the first one, but in the same measure its importance is inferior to the first one as for development of thought. Only the Type III of orientation in the subject leads to clearly understand the relationship between the general patterns of things with scientific knowledge of them and, at the same time, the relationship between learning and development of thought, for only with this type of teaching, the programme of instruction includes the formation of generalized patterns of reality which, by study, become the generalized and unified patterns of individual actions and new structures of thought. It is possible therefore, that the Type III is the kind of learning, theorically admitted by Piaget, in which the acquisition of knowledge goes side by side with intellectual development, though the two process remain, of course, the different sides of the same process. This type of learning, however, has been hitherto unknown, whereas with all other types of learning, there is a wide gap between the acquisition of knowledge and the development of though, so that the relationship between them becomes so remote and vague that it allows a great variety of interpretations. Hence it is clear that in the study of intellectual development, the description of separate stages, even if this description is made by such a penetrating master of psychological analysis as Piaget, states merely the achieved conditions and cannot give sufficient basis to judge of process of development or dynamic forces. At the present time, only the organization of the stage-by-stage formation of mental actions and concepts (characteristic of the period of development upon which our interest is centered), through the use of Type III of orientation in the subject, does open a way to studying intellectual development in its dynamics, in the operation of its motivational forces and its emerging mechanisms.
149:VYGOTSKY, L.S. *Umstvennoje rasvitije detey v processe obuchenija* (Intellectual development of children in process of learning). Moscow-Leningrad : State Learning-Pedagogical Publishing House, 1935.
151:ZHDAN, A.N. Opyt primenenia psikhologicheskoy teorii o tipakh ucheniya k postroeniyu uchebnogo predmeta : morfologii russkogo yazyka (An essay of applying the psychological theory of the types of learning to the construction of the learning subject: morphology of Russian language). Abstract of doctoral dissertation, Moscow, 1968.

toolUseResult.stdout:
33:The study of the intellectual development of the child was hitherto limited to cross-section methods that allowed only to show the level of development achieved. We did not know how concepts are formed in the child; we just gave him tasks requiring the formation of a concept and then found out what he could perform. The method of determining the "zone of proximal development" by the difference in the results between independent action and joint child-and-adult activity merely complicated the scheme, without removing this basic limitation. By building up a serial row of such static indexes, we traced the curve of intellectual development, while its dynamic forces and its very necessity remained concealed. Taking into account various circumstances, one could have very different conceptions about these things. Such conceptions were in fact suggested, for example, by Vygotsky ( 1935 ) and Piaget ( 1953 ). Vygotsky said that "learning pushes ahead leading development in its wake", while Piaget asserts that intellectual development takes place spontaneously and that an efficient learning is possible only on the basis and in the measure of the level of development reached. The possibility of such a difference shows up clearly that the problem of method is the very first problem in studying the intellectual development of the child. In this perspective, we have better start with the description of how our method (Galperin, 1959 ) was formed and how it brought us one day to face the problem of mental development of the child, a subject we had been dodging stubbornly all the time.
45:## THREE BASIC TYPES OF LEARNING
47:After the investigations of W. Kohler ( 1917 ), we can no longer doubt that reasoning in action has completely objective features. Its measure is found in how much the child is orienting the action by its substantial, external, objective conditions. Naturally, we wished to ensure reasoning in action as much as possible and we came, one day, so near to the solution of this problem that we obtained an altogether new type of process. The trials and errors, so characteristic of the traditional formation of actions, have become rare and incidental, the duration of formation has been sharply curtailed (at the expense of most protracted and difficult period of establishing the right action), the deviations of quality in separate executions have become insignificant; transfer has brown substantially, even the attitude of the individual towards the process of learning has changed. The new way of forming actions and concepts was named Type II, in order to mark its difference from the usual, commonly known Type I which historically came first. A comparison between Type I and Type II conditions will easily explain, at the same time, the mechanisms of Type I and its hitherto predominance in learning : the incompleteness of conditions required for the correct performance of a new assignment makes it unavoidable and even advantageous in a way. At the same time, another type of instruction, Type III, was discovered; some of its details will be discussed later.
49:This is how the theory of the three basic types of mental action and concept formation was elaborated. Its most general assumption is that the type of orientation in a matter, the process of learning, the quality of its results, the attitude toward the matter and the process of learning are closely intertwined and form an unitary process, the process of learning. The decisive part in this process is the type of orientation in the matter; that is why we say "type of orientation" for meaning the corresponding type of learning and "type of learning" for meaning the corresponding type of orientation. The theory of basic types of learning is so important for the "learning and development" problem that we feel justified to discuss it at some length. To simplify the explanation, let us introduce some notions (Galperin, 1966 ).
55:***Type I :*** $Oa < Sp$. This means that the individual lacks some of the signs that are essential for the correct performance of a new action; he is unable to reconstruct them without help; if he well carries out such a reconstruction, the formation turns out into Type II. There where the signs are missing, the individual has only got one way open to him, that of blind trials. In this case, errors are unavoidable, and yet incidental success is possible. These successful actions, however, will be noticed only for their result, for often they are far from the best and, what is more important, they will not always lead to the discovery of their objective conditions. That is why successful actions, purposeful as they are, remain, for most part, unreasoned. Correct actions are formed slowly; the process is clearly divided into two phases of unequal duration : the phase in which the general structure of the action is established and the phase in which the action is perfected. In the long run, the action may reach its utmost precision, limited only by physiological possibilities, but as much as a considerable part of its objective conditions remains unknown to the individual, it is highly subject to changes with the even unimportant changes in conditions. This is the origin of fluctuations of quality that are permanently observed in individual performance and out of proportion with the changes in conditions and that also the image, as it were, of spontaneous fluctuations in the characteristics of individual executions. The degree of success in learning greatly varies among the individuals. A transfer to new assignments is very unsatisfactory in a majority of individuals. Since separate executions and the formation of action on the whole are mainly controlled by the final result, the attitude of the individual towards the process of learning is determined by his attitude towards this result and is of purely utilitarian nature.
57:***Type II :*** $Oa = Sp$. In this case, the individual has a complete system of signs at his disposal to perform correctly a new assignment which he follows without failure; otherwise, the return to Type I occurs. Thus the first, longest and most difficult period of acquisition of the action becomes unnecessary. This means that the process of learning is already curtailed considerably. In Type II, every link of the action is correlated to its objective condition, and *in this measure* the action is not only successful, but also reasoned. Very soon it reaches the same physiological possibilities, but now the characteristics of separate performances are far more stable, because the objective signs of the action are very prominent among all other environmental components. The degree of success in learning among the individuals becomes more uniform; group success shows greater uniformity at a higher level. Transfer grows considerably, compared to that in Type I. It shows much fluctuation in different assignments, but even at its best it remains by far incomplete. Since the conditions of the correct performance are given to the individual, his interest is growing into a successful and ever more perfect execution of the assignment, in the very process of learning. This is precisely an interest in the success of his action only, as his attitude towards its result and towards learning in general remains utilitarian. But the final result loses its importance as the only criterion, while the apparent significance of singling out the conditions of action and therefore, of theoretical research, is met with increasing recognition.
59:The advantages of the Type II, as compared to Type I are quite evident and important, especially when the task is limited to mastering separate assignments. But at school, learning assignments usually refer to a certain sphere and come in a long succession. In respect for such a succession, the basis short-coming of Type II learning becomes clearly indicated : the $Oa$ for *every* new assignment has to be indicated from the beginning (transfer remains substantially incomplete); it is to be found empirically, by finding out how much the suggested signs will eliminate errors made by the individuals. This shortcoming of Type II led to the new task of teaching the child how to construct a complete $Oa$ for new assignments, at least for a definite and limited sphere. Evidently, the desired result will be obtained when the child is provided with such a method of analysis to construct, without assistance, a complete $Oa$ for any phenomenon in this sphere. It is also clear that such an analysis must bear on — ( 1 ) the "basic units" of the material of the sphere in question and — ( 2 ) the general rules of their combination in concrete phenomena. In accordance with this, when faced with the first objects from a new sphere, the individual acquires two methods : the method of singling out the basic units from the concrete objects and the method of caracterizing their combinations in these objects. The results is a complete $Oa$ of the object at hand which is different from the empirically constructed complete $Oa$ of Type II, by its rational origin. Such a complete and rational $Oa$ becomes the basis for the acquisition of actions and concepts related to the studied object.
61:## LEARNING BY THE METHOD OF TYPE III
63:The kind of instruction which we called Type III was successfully implemented at first in teaching writing of letters and words. Even in comparison with Type II, the results were proved to be so much better in all respects that we decided to generalize it in applying this type of instruction to the considerably different material : grammatical analysis of words, initial arithmetical operations and concepts.
67:In teaching writing of letters, the contour was chosen as the object to be studied and mastered, or, to be more exact, its shape, size and localization (Galperin, 1958 ). We took the "line of unchanging direction" as unit of contour : at points where the line or its direction began to change, division dots were put on so that the line was located between two such dots. The localization of the dots themselves was indicated at the crosspoints of the "slanting" line with the "reclining" one (the vertical and horizontal lines of the page). Six-year-old children were taught to find the division dots, to supply them with *verbal* characteristics (by describing their localization on the network of coordinates in the page), to transfer the key dots, in accordance with these verbal characteristics, to the clean space on the line and then, following the dots, to reproduce the contour. All these actions requiring orientation in the structure and localization of the contour were transferred by stages to the mental plane where they were brought up to the level of automatism. After that, the children were writing letters and words without thinking of how they had done it. After such a learning for the first few letters, the children mastered independently the writing of the rest of the letters of the Russian alphabet (totalizing 58 letters, capital letters included). During control tests, they had no difficulty in analyzing and reproducing latin letters, Armenian and Arabic writing, shorthand characters, drawings, sketches; they could trace trajectories described by only moving bodies on a plane. Due to the clear arrangement of objects in space, they could count things much better. In a word, transfer was not only complete in the first selected sphere (writing of Russian letters) but reached far beyond its borders covering all the assignments where it was important to take into account the arrangement of objects on a plane. The method of teaching is itself noteworthy. The child was asked not only to show, but also to prove that the line changes at this point exactly and not at an adjacent one, to mark the place of a key dot by drawing auxiliary lines if necessary, and to describe verbally in such a way that another child could indicate its exact localization, etc. In all instances, we resorted to the judgement of other children and asked the individual to describe each element so that it would have the same meaning for all. In other words, the children were systematically taught to develop an objective awareness of the plane structure and of the characteristics of its elements, and to use this knowledge for the recognition and reproduction of concrete graphical objects.
73:This analysis, carried out on different words from different parts of speech, yields a general scheme of the word, and of all the words, which is a common carrier of a somewhat intricate, but clearly outlined system of possible combinations for different units of information. This scheme becomes an instrument of speech activity, while speech itself turns into an active process of using different semes for the desired presentation of things. In this meaning, language becomes indeed not only an instrument of activity, but activity itself (it is Humboldt's idea, though without Humboldt's mysticism). It is noteworthy that this way of understanding the language, *i.e.*, from the aspect of people's consciousness fixed in it, "language consciousness", simplifies considerably the task of characterizing language phenomena and makes it possible to present them with more regularity. This, in its turn, provides an opportunity to ensure a correct orientation among these phenomena and their mastering by means achieving grammatical tasks. This way of correct grammar is directly applied to written and oral speech, without satiety and with ever increasing interest. The initial and basic principle of discrimination of the smallest units of information leads in an easily understood way to a quick and assured formation of the "feeling of language" which can be extensively transferred to the learning of foreign languages, the study of literature and precise formulation of logical thought. This way of learning a language is not merely acquisition of the rules of grammatically correct speech, but also discovery of a new world, the world of people's consciousness concerning things, people and their relations. It is, at the same time, a new structure of speech thinking supported by the basic patterns which have been deposited in this language consciousness.

Of course, these general patterns are present, but in a hidden manner, in the knowledge acquired through the Type I and Type II. But in this condition, the practical application of knowledge can result in a spontaneous, "intuitional" approximation to these general patterns and in the correspondingly spontaneous reorganization of thought. But it occurs for different individuals with varying degree of success, and even when thought begins to follow these patterns in some degree, they are still treated in a subconscious manner and remain within the framework of the empirical material from which they emerged.
129:Thus all acquirements of learning can be divided into two unequal parts. The first one consists of new general patterns of things which condition a new way of seeing things and thinking about them; the other one consists of concrete facts and laws found in the sphere under study, that is to say, the concrete material of a science. In its totality, the second part has more weight than the first one, but in the same measure its importance is inferior to the first one as for development of thought. Only the Type III of orientation in the subject leads to clearly understand the relationship between the general patterns of things with scientific knowledge of them and, at the same time, the relationship between learning and development of thought, for only with this type of teaching, the programme of instruction includes the formation of generalized patterns of reality which, by study, become the generalized and unified patterns of individual actions and new structures of thought. It is possible therefore, that the Type III is the kind of learning, theorically admitted by Piaget, in which the acquisition of knowledge goes side by side with intellectual development, though the two process remain, of course, the different sides of the same process. This type of learning, however, has been hitherto unknown, whereas with all other types of learning, there is a wide gap between the acquisition of knowledge and the development of though, so that the relationship between them becomes so remote and vague that it allows a great variety of interpretations. Hence it is clear that in the study of intellectual development, the description of separate stages, even if this description is made by such a penetrating master of psychological analysis as Piaget, states merely the achieved conditions and cannot give sufficient basis to judge of process of development or dynamic forces. At the present time, only the organization of the stage-by-stage formation of mental actions and concepts (characteristic of the period of development upon which our interest is centered), through the use of Type III of orientation in the subject, does open a way to studying intellectual development in its dynamics, in the operation of its motivational forces and its emerging mechanisms.
149:VYGOTSKY, L.S. *Umstvennoje rasvitije detey v processe obuchenija* (Intellectual development of children in process of learning). Moscow-Leningrad : State Learning-Pedagogical Publishing House, 1935.
151:ZHDAN, A.N. Opyt primenenia psikhologicheskoy teorii o tipakh ucheniya k postroeniyu uchebnogo predmeta : morfologii russkogo yazyka (An essay of applying the psychological theory of the types of learning to the construction of the learning subject: morphology of Russian language). Abstract of doctoral dissertation, Moscow, 1968.

lastPrompt: Galperin 那篇儿童智力发展的论文为什么不构成有效落点？

content.text:
我错了——刚才那句「构不成有效落点」只看了标题就下了判断。读完正文后，更准确的描述是：

**Galperin 1968 的实际内容**：这篇论文讲心智动作分阶段形成理论（Type I/II/III 三种基本学习类型），核心比较的是它们在 transfer（迁移）上的差异。Type I 是「试错」型学习，定向条件不全，学习者只能盲试；Galperin 的实验结论是 Type I 的迁移在大多数个体身上「very unsatisfactory」。Type II 给齐定向条件，迁移「grows considerably... but even at its best remains by far incomplete」。Type III 教学生自建定向，文中报告儿童学了俄文字母后能直接迁移到拉丁字母、亚美尼亚字、阿拉伯字、速记、草图，「transfer was not only complete in the first selected sphere but reached far beyond its borders」。

**与你的问题的关系**，分两层看：

1. 如果你要的是机器学习意义上强化学习（MDP、reward、policy、PPO/RLHF/RLVR 之类）在 benchmark 上的泛化表现，这篇答不了，它不在那个语境里。
2. 如果你的问题底层关心的是「以试错和结果反馈为主要信号的学习，泛化能力如何」，Galperin 的 Type I 描述就是发展心理学版本的同一问题，他给的答案是「迁移很差，原因是学习者从未把『行动的客观条件』显式表达出来」，而解决方案 Type III 的关键是先把对象的基本单元和组合规则讲清楚再让学生执行。这与 ML 圈关于「纯 RL 还是 SFT + RL」「RL 是否需要密集监督信号或显式 reasoning trace 才能泛化」的讨论结构上贴得相当近。

所以是否构成落点，取决于你这个问题真正想问的是哪一层。要我按第二种读法来答（用 Galperin 这一篇作源料），还是补源料后再答第一种？

input.command: ls mapping/concepts/ | grep -iE "type|orientation|transfer|定向|迁移|学习"
input.description: 查找 mapping 里的相关概念页

content.text:
按 [[mapping/entities/piotr-galperin.md|Galperin]] 在 [[Library/Towards Research of the Intellectual Development of the Child.md|Galperin 1968]] 里给出的回答：试错型学习（他称之为 Type I）的迁移在大多数学习者身上都很差。

他的论证从「定向（orientation）的完整性」切入。Type I 的处境是 $Oa < Sp$，即学习者掌握的定向信号集小于正确执行新任务所需的本质信号集。在这种条件下，错误不可避免，偶然成功也确实会出现，但成功的动作只因其结果而被注意到，行动的客观条件并未被辨认出来。学习者由此走出来的曲线是先建立行动的大致结构、再不断精修；精度可以最终逼近生理极限，可是「相当一部分客观条件对个体而言始终未知」，所以条件上的细小变化都会让执行质量大幅波动。Galperin 由此上升为对 Type I 的整体判断：「迁移到新任务的能力在大多数个体身上都很不令人满意」。

他给的原因不是训练量不够，而是控制信号的性质决定的。单次执行与整体学习「主要由最终结果来控制」，学习者对学习过程本身的态度也因此停留在纯粹功利层面（只在乎结果对不对）。结果信号能告诉学习者做对没做对，告诉不了为什么做对；于是支撑「为什么对」的那套客观条件就以隐含、不可言明的方式留在个体行为里，迁移到新任务时无法被主动调用。

Type II 补齐了执行所需的完整信号集，迁移随之「显著增长」，但 Galperin 紧接着指出这条路有天花板：每个新任务的 $Oa$ 仍要从头构造，迁移「即使在最好情形下也远远不完整」。真正能远迁移的是 Type III，关键不在多给信号，而在教会学习者从该领域的对象中自行提取「基本单元」与「组合规则」，从而独立地为任何新对象构造完整定向。论文报告了一组对照：六岁儿童按 Type III 学完俄文字母后，控制实验里能直接迁移到拉丁字母、亚美尼亚字、阿拉伯字、速记符号、草图，乃至追踪动体在平面上的轨迹，「迁移不仅在所选定领域内完整，而且远远超出该领域的边界」。

把三条放在一起，Galperin 对你这个问题给的根本回答是：迁移能力由定向的完整性与理性化程度决定，不由训练强度或试错次数决定。只靠结果反馈、不显式提取对象的基本单元与组合规则的学习，泛化天花板很低；要获得远迁移，必须让一般 pattern 从隐含状态被显式提取出来，并成为学习者主动用以解析新对象的思维结构。这是 [[mapping/concepts/stage-by-stage-formation-of-mental-actions.md|心智动作分阶段形成理论]] 在 1968 那篇里最直接的承诺。
